title: Vegetarian Sausage Roll
subtitle: Chestnut mushroom, walnut, and herb filling in flaky puff pastry
excerpt: Vegetarian sausage rolls use a dense mushroom and walnut mixture that holds its shape inside the pastry and has enough fat content to stay moist. They bake identically to the meat version and hold up well at room temperature.
sourceNotes: Method synthesised from British vegetarian cookery tradition. The chestnut mushroom and nut filling as a pastry wrap is documented in British vegetarian cooking from the 1980s onward.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze assembled, uncooked rolls on a tray until solid, then bag. Bake from frozen at 190°C for 35 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: Double or triple the batch; freeze in portions.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Assemble up to 4 hours ahead and refrigerate unbaked. Brush with egg just before baking.

glossaryTerms.definition: To fry food quickly in a small amount of fat over a high heat, tossing or stirring so that it cooks evenly and browns rather than steams. From the French for 'jumped', referring to the movement of the food in the pan.

content.text: Vegetarian sausage rolls succeed when the filling is dense and well-seasoned enough to hold its shape inside the pastry and carry flavour through to the last bite. Chestnut mushrooms provide the meaty texture; walnuts give fat and body; breadcrumbs bind and absorb moisture during baking. The filling needs to be properly cooked before rolling, not assembled raw.
Heat a wide pan over a high heat. Add the {{chestnut-mushrooms}} of finely chopped mushrooms in a single layer and
without stirring for 3 to 4 minutes until golden and all moisture has evaporated. Any remaining water will make the filling soggy. Reduce the heat to medium.

Add the {{onion}} diced onion and cook for 5 minutes until soft. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic, thyme leaves, and sage, and stir for 1 minute. Add the soy sauce and stir until absorbed. Remove from the heat.

Transfer to a bowl. Stir in the {{walnuts}} of chopped walnuts and {{breadcrumbs-fresh}} of fresh breadcrumbs. Taste and season well with salt and pepper. The mixture should be cohesive and hold together when squeezed; if it is too wet, add more breadcrumbs. Leave to cool completely.
Heat the oven to 200°C. Line a baking tray with baking paper. Unroll the puff pastry on a lightly floured surface and cut in half lengthways to give two long strips.

Divide the filling into two halves. Roll each half into a log and lay it along the centre of one pastry strip. Brush one long edge with beaten egg and fold the other edge over to enclose the filling. Press firmly to seal and turn seam-side down.

Brush the tops with egg wash. Cut each roll into 6 pieces for large rolls or 12 for cocktail size. Place on the lined tray with the cuts facing upward. Score the top of each roll twice with a sharp knife.
Bake for 20 to 25 minutes until deep golden. Rest for 5 minutes before serving.

items.symptom: Filling is wet and the pastry is soggy
items.cause: Mushrooms were not cooked long enough to evaporate all their moisture.
items.fix: Cook the mushrooms until the pan is completely dry before adding the onion. The filling must be dry enough to hold its shape when squeezed between your fingers.

items.cause: Not enough breadcrumbs to bind, or the filling was too hot when rolled.
items.fix: Add more breadcrumbs until the mixture coheres when pressed. Always cool the filling to room temperature before rolling in the pastry.

items.cause: The seam was not sealed firmly enough.
items.fix: Press the egg-washed join firmly before turning seam-side down. The weight of the roll on the seam also helps keep it closed.

items.cause: Underseasoned, or the soy sauce wasn't sufficient.
items.fix: Taste the filling before rolling. It should be well-seasoned and slightly salty. Add more soy sauce or salt and pepper as needed.

content.text: The vegetarian sausage roll became a standard party and picnic item in Britain from the 1980s, when vegetarian food moved into mainstream catering. Early vegetarian versions often used eggs, cheese, or nut roast-style mixtures. The mushroom-walnut combination became the standard because it can be seasoned as deeply as the pork original and holds its shape under baking without drying out. The rolls behave the same as meat versions on a buffet table.
